ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken curry with rice — composite dish; FoodEx2 base term for mixed rice-based meat dish used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Singaporean Malay curry diced chicken rice. A0CKN (mixed dish, cereal-based with meat) used as closest defensible anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of curry diced chicken rice is approximately 400 g, comprising a serving of steamed white rice topped with diced chicken pieces in coconut-milk curry sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Curry Chicken Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (6.25 g/100g), trans fat (0.13 g/100g), and vitamin C (2.5 mg/100g) were originally tagged to GrabFood in the input record at confidence 0.70; GrabFood is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. These three values are therefore retained as provisional estimates from the input record pending verification against an approved primary source, and are flagged in notes. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water are not reported in the HPB entry for this dish and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean Malay Curry Diced Chicken Rice in any approved source. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on white rice GI of 72 reported in Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021: a systematic review. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition. 2021;114(5):1625-1632. doi:10.1093/ajcn/nqab233. The white rice GI of 72 is moderated downward to 65 to account for the protein (4.96 g/100g), fat (4.0 g/100g), and dietary fibre (1.0 g/100g) contributions from the curry sauce and chicken, consistent with published mixed-meal GI attenuation evidence in the same peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (60.48 g from 400 g portion) / 100 = 39.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 324 mg/100g with insufficient potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: At 324 mg sodium per 100 g, a standard 400 g plate delivers approximately 1296 mg sodium — over half the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg — and the potassium content of 182 mg/100g is insufficient to meaningfully offset this load; patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt curry preparation and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base, elevated saturated fat, and low dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base carries an estimated GI of 65 (medium-high) and delivers approximately 60 g of carbohydrate per standard 400 g plate, while dietary fibre at only 1.0 g/100g provides minimal glycaemic modulation and saturated fat from coconut milk further impairs insulin sensitivity; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request brown rice substitution, reduced coconut milk, and limit portion to no more than 200–250 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The coconut-milk curry base contributes significant saturated fat and a 400 g plate delivers approximately 1296 mg sodium, both of which are established cardiovascular risk factors; patients with cardiovascular disease should request reduced coconut milk preparation, limit portion size, and pair with a high-fibre vegetable side to partially offset the atherogenic lipid and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices present but saturated fat and sodium are concerns
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Turmeric (curcumin), ginger (gingerols), lemongrass, and garlic in the curry paste provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit per EULAR dietary guidance, but saturated fat from coconut milk promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity and sodium at 324 mg/100g may worsen RA-related tissue oedema; patients should request reduced coconut milk and salt, and monitor symptoms if sensitive to nightshade ingredients (chilli, potato).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Curry Diced Chicken Rice (Malay: Nasi Ayam Kari; Chinese: 咖喱鸡丁饭) is a popular Singaporean Malay rice dish consisting of bite-sized diced chicken pieces simmered in a rich, aromatic coconut-milk curry sauce served over steamed white rice. The curry sauce is prepared with a spice paste of turmeric, lemongrass, galangal, ginger, garlic, shallots, and dried chilli, giving the dish its characteristic golden-orange colour, warm heat, and complex layered aroma. The chicken is tender and absorbs the curry flavour deeply during stewing. The rice is fluffy and slightly sticky, providing a neutral base that balances the richness of the sauce. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 116 kcal, 4.96 g protein, 4.0 g fat, and 15.12 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 324 mg. A standard 400 g plate delivers approximately 464 kcal, 19.8 g protein, 16 g fat, and 60.5 g carbohydrate. The dish is a staple at Singaporean hawker centres, food courts, and economic rice (cai png) stalls, commonly consumed at lunch and dinner. Clinically, the dish is of high concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its high-GI white rice base (estimated GI 65) and low fibre content (1.0 g/100g), and of high concern for cardiovascular disease due to its saturated fat load from coconut milk. Sodium at 324 mg/100g places it in the moderate concern category for hypertension. The dish is halal-certified in most hawker contexts and is gluten-free and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in curry diced chicken rice?
Is curry chicken rice suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95 with exact dish match. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C are provisional estimates from the input record pending approved-source verification and are flagged accordingly. GI estimated from peer-reviewed approved source (Atkinson et al. 2021, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition) with documented analogous estimation basis. Multilingual names verified for Singaporean Malay cuisine category.
notes: NUTRIENT SOURCE COMPLIANCE: Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (approved primary source). saturated_fat_g (6.25), trans_fat_g (0.13), and vitamin_c_mg (2.5) were tagged to GrabFood in the input record at confidence 0.70. GrabFood is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. These three values are retained as provisional estimates from the input record and are flagged for mandatory verification against an approved primary source (HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central) before production use. DATA INCONSISTENCY FLAG: saturated_fat_g (6.25) exceeds total fat_g (4.0) — saturated fat cannot exceed total fat. This is a data inconsistency inherited from the input record. The HPB-sourced total fat value of 4.0 g/100g is considered the more reliable anchor. This inconsistency must be resolved during approved-source verification. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Singaporean Malay curry rice dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for Curry Diced Chicken Rice as of 2026. GI NOTE: GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI of 72 (Atkinson et al. 2021, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, doi:10.1093/ajcn/nqab233) moderated by mixed-meal composition; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in any approved source. PORTION NOTE: Two portion entries in the input (550 g and 400 g) were both flagged is_default=true; 400 g has been adopted as the standard serving size as the more conservative and clinically representative value for a main dish portion.
